title: Red Wine on Leather
timeEstimate: Pro-lane; home is blot + condition only
stainChemistry: Leather is animal hide preserved by tanning + dyed + (usually) topcoated with a polymer finish. Two leather variants matter: (1) FINISHED / pigmented leather — has a polymer topcoat that resists most spills; common on furniture, handbags, jackets. (2) ANILINE / unfinished — topcoat-free, dyed-through, much more porous; even water can leave permanent marks. Red wine anthocyanins penetrate through wear spots in finished leather, and soak into aniline directly. The CRITICAL leather rule: NO oxidizers — peroxide, OxiClean, chlorine bleach all permanently strip the finish + dye + natural oils. Only safe path is mild soap + thorough condition, OR a leather specialist with sodium hydrosulfite (reducing bleach, NOT oxidizer) + leather conditioner. Most home advice for wine on leather is wrong because it carries over fabric chemistry that destroys leather.
whyThisWorks: Quick blot prevents wine from soaking past the topcoat into the dyed layer. Mild soap + minimum water + thorough conditioning preserves the hide. The CHEMISTRY that lifts tannin (sodium hydrosulfite — reducing bleach) is leather-safe but pro-only. Home oxidizer attempts strip the finish in seconds. The honest home protocol: blot quickly, use minimum gentle cleaner, condition immediately, escalate fast.
defaultAssumption: Finished/pigmented leather with topcoat. Aniline / unfinished is pro-only from minute one — even water rings it. Most furniture / handbag / shoe leather is finished; high-end aniline is rarer but exists.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Place a single drop of water on a hidden leather area. If it beads on top = finished/pigmented (topcoat present, proceed). If it darkens / soaks in immediately = aniline / unfinished (call leather specialist now, no DIY). Bead-then-soak after a few minutes = semi-aniline (treat as aniline).

spottingProtocol.equipment: Clean white cloth or absorbent paper
spottingProtocol.instruction: Press a clean white cloth onto the wine pool. Lift straight up, press fresh cloth, repeat until no more wine transfers. Do not rub — rubbing pushes wine through the topcoat into the dyed layer where it cannot be reached without re-dyeing.

spottingProtocol.equipment: 1–2 drops mild soap (Dawn) in a cup of warm water, white cloth wrung nearly dry
spottingProtocol.instruction: Mix 1–2 drops of mild dish soap in a cup of warm water. Dampen a white cloth, wring NEARLY DRY (no dripping). Wipe the stain area in gentle circles, outside-in. Do not soak the leather. Wipe with a plain-water-damp cloth to clear soap residue.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Sodium hydrosulfite per pro reference + leather conditioner
spottingProtocol.instruction: Pro-only step. Reducing bleach (NOT oxidizer) lifts oxidized anthocyanin from finished leather without stripping. Requires controlled application + immediate conditioning. Home cannot replicate safely.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Lexol, Bickmore, or pro leather conditioner
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply leather conditioner with a soft cloth. Cleaning strips natural oils — un-conditioned leather cracks. This step is non-negotiable after any wet treatment, even minor.

steps: 1. Identify leather type — aniline = pro from minute one.
3. Mild soap on barely-damp cloth, outside-in.
4. Sodium hydrosulfite for residual tannin (reducing bleach, NOT oxidizer).
products: Mild dish soap (Dawn, 1–2 drops in water)
professionalProtocol.temperature: ambient — no heat (heat dries out leather, causes cracking)
warnings: NEVER oxidizers — peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine bleach strip finish + dye + oils
NEVER acetone — destroys leather finish on contact
NEVER excess water — warps leather, causes spotting
NEVER skip conditioner after cleaning — leads to cracking
Aniline / unfinished leather = pro from minute one

homeSolutions.agent: Identify Your Leather First — One Drop of Water
homeSolutions.instruction: Before doing anything else, drop ONE bead of water on a hidden leather area (under a cushion, inside a flap, behind a strap). Wait 30 seconds. If it BEADS on top = finished leather, has a topcoat, you can proceed carefully below. If it SOAKS IN immediately and darkens the leather = aniline / unfinished — STOP. Do nothing. Aniline leather is pro-only from minute one — even water rings it permanently. Anything in between (semi-aniline) — treat as aniline, go pro.

homeSolutions.agent: What NOT To Do — The Bad-Advice List
homeSolutions.instruction: The internet's worst leather advice: hydrogen peroxide, OxiClean, chlorine bleach, baking soda paste, hairspray, white wine, salt. ALL of these strip the leather's finish, dye, and natural oils — usually permanently. The damage from these is much worse than the wine. Also do NOT use acetone, undiluted alcohol, or anything labeled "degreaser." Your safe toolkit is exactly four things: clean white cloth, mild dish soap (1–2 drops), water, and a leather conditioner. Nothing else.

homeSolutions.agent: Blot the Wine Immediately — Press, Don't Rub
homeSolutions.instruction: Press a clean white cloth straight down on the wine. Lift straight up, press fresh part of the cloth, repeat. Do NOT wipe or rub — rubbing pushes wine through any wear spots in the topcoat into the dyed layer underneath, where home cleaning can't reach it. Just press and lift. Get as much wet wine off the surface as possible before any chemistry.

homeSolutions.agent: Mild Soap on a BARELY-Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix 1 to 2 drops of Dawn (just two drops, not a tablespoon) in a cup of warm water. Dampen a clean white cloth in it and WRING IT OUT until it's barely damp — no dripping, no wet smear. Wipe the stained area in gentle circles, working from the OUTSIDE of the stain inward. Then wipe again with a plain-water-damp cloth to remove the soap. The leather should never be visibly wet — barely damp throughout.

Even gentle cleaning strips leather's natural oils. If you skip this step, the leather will dry out and crack over weeks. Apply a leather conditioner (Lexol or Bickmore at any drugstore / hardware store) with a soft cloth, work it in per the label, let it absorb. This isn't optional — it's the difference between a clean spot and a dry, cracked leather over time.

homeSolutions.agent: Pro for Anything Remaining — Don't Repeat at Home
homeSolutions.instruction: If a shadow remains after one careful home pass, take it to a leather care specialist. NOT a regular dry cleaner — leather is its own discipline. Tell them: "Red wine on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, age [hours / days], one home pass with mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol used." Pros use sodium hydrosulfite (REDUCING bleach, leather-safe) + re-dye if needed — chemistry home cannot do safely.

steps: 1. Identify leather: drop water test. Aniline = pro from minute one.
2. NO oxidizers, NO acetone, NO alcohol, NO baking soda.
4. Mild soap (1–2 drops Dawn) on a BARELY-damp cloth, outside-in.
5. Plain-water damp wipe to clear soap.
6. Leather conditioner — mandatory after any wet treatment.
7. Residual = pro leather specialist. NO second home pass.
products: Mild dish soap (Dawn — 1–2 drops, no more)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if leather is aniline / unfinished, if a stain remains after one careful pass, or if anyone else has already applied oxidizer / acetone / alcohol.
warnings: NEVER peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine bleach on leather — strip finish + dye permanently
NEVER acetone — destroys leather on contact
NEVER excess water — warps leather + spotting
NEVER baking soda paste — abrasive + alkaline + dries leather
NEVER skip conditioner — leather cracks over weeks

neverDo: Never any oxidizer (peroxide / OxiClean / chlorine) on leather
Never acetone on leather — destroys finish
Never skip conditioner after wet cleaning
fiberSensitivities: Finished / pigmented leather: tolerates mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner
Aniline / unfinished: pro-only — even water rings it
Semi-aniline: treat as aniline, go pro
Suede: different protocol entirely (no liquid at all)
Patent leather: own protocol — no abrasion

materialWarnings: Aniline = pro from minute one (even water rings)
Conditioner after every wet treatment, mandatory
Leather chemistry is its own discipline — fabric chemistry doesn't transfer

escalation.when: Aniline / unfinished leather (always), shadow remaining after one home pass, or anyone has applied oxidizer / acetone / alcohol previously.
escalation.whatToTell: "Red wine on [finished / aniline / unknown] leather, age [hours / days], one home pass with mild soap + barely-damp cloth + conditioner. No oxidizer, no acetone, no alcohol used [or: someone applied X before I got here]."
escalation.specialistType: leather care specialist (NOT a regular dry cleaner — leather is its own discipline; ask if they handle red wine on aniline leather)

contextQuestions: Did the water-drop test bead, soak, or in-between?
Furniture / handbag / jacket / shoes / car seat?
Color — light leather shows residual stain more
Has anyone applied peroxide / OxiClean / acetone / hairspray / baking soda?
How valuable / how visible is the area?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Aniline leather is the unforgiving variant — common on high-end furniture and luxury handbags. Car interior leather is usually finished + heavily topcoated, more forgiving. Light-colored leather shows residual better than dark — set expectations accordingly. Any prior application of harsh chemistry usually strips finish before the cleaner sees it; honest disclosure changes the pro's plan.
